fields.name: Anxiety Disorders
fields.definition: A group of mental health conditions characterized by excessive, persistent fear or worry that is difficult to control and interferes significantly with daily life. Unlike the normal anxiety people feel before a presentation or a medical procedure, anxiety disorders involve disproportionate dread that does not go away and can worsen over time. They are the most common class of mental disorders in the United States.
fields.example: A person with an anxiety disorder may avoid driving on highways, calling strangers on the phone, or attending social events — even when they logically know the threat is minimal.

fields.name: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder characterized by excessive, hard-to-control anxiety and worry about a wide variety of topics — work, health, family, everyday matters — occurring more days than not for at least 6 months. The worry is out of proportion to the actual likelihood or impact of the anticipated events, and is accompanied by physical symptoms such as muscle tension, fatigue, irritability, difficulty concentrating, and sleep disturbance.
fields.example: Persistently worrying about whether family members are safe, whether finances are secure, and whether work performance is adequate — even when no actual problems exist.

fields.name: Panic Disorder
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder characterized by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks — sudden surges of intense fear or discomfort that peak within minutes and include symptoms such as racing heart, sweating, trembling, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, and fear of losing control or dying. To be diagnosed, a person must experience ongoing worry about future attacks or make significant behavioral changes (such as avoidance) to prevent them.
fields.example: A person who has experienced several unexpected panic attacks begins avoiding public spaces, fearing another attack will occur with no way to escape.

fields.name: Social Anxiety Disorder
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder characterized by intense, persistent fear of being watched, judged, or humiliated in social or performance situations. The fear is out of proportion to the actual threat and leads to avoidance or endurance with extreme distress. It goes far beyond ordinary shyness and can interfere with work, school, and relationships. Physical symptoms — blushing, sweating, trembling, nausea — are common in feared situations.
fields.example: Avoiding work presentations entirely, eating alone to avoid being watched, or rehearsing conversations for hours due to fear of saying something embarrassing.

fields.name: Specific Phobia
fields.definition: An anxiety disorder involving an intense, irrational fear of a specific object or situation — such as flying, heights, spiders, blood, or injections — that is out of proportion to any actual danger. Exposure to the feared stimulus almost always triggers immediate anxiety. The person typically avoids the trigger or endures it with intense distress. The phobia must cause significant impairment or distress to qualify as a disorder.
fields.example: A person with a specific phobia of flying declines promotions requiring travel and drives 14 hours to avoid a 2-hour flight.

fields.body: When the brain perceives a threat, the amygdala triggers the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, releasing adrenaline (epinephrine) and cortisol into the bloodstream. Heart rate and breathing accelerate to deliver more oxygen to muscles. Blood is redirected from digestion to large muscle groups. Pupils dilate, sweating increases, and pain perception is dulled — all preparing the body to fight or flee. In anxiety disorders, this system activates in response to non-threatening situations and remains stuck in an elevated state, causing chronic physical symptoms such as muscle tension, fatigue, and insomnia.
fields.name: The Fight-or-Flight Response
fields.example: Heart pounding and palms sweating before a routine phone call — the body responds as if to a physical threat even though no danger is present.

fields.body: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for anxiety begins by helping the person identify distorted, threat-amplifying thought patterns (cognitive restructuring). The therapist and patient examine the evidence for and against catastrophic predictions. Behavioral experiments test whether feared outcomes actually occur. Gradual exposure to feared situations (exposure therapy) is central: the person deliberately encounters avoided triggers in a controlled way, allowing the anxiety response to peak and subside without escape — a process called habituation. Over repeated exposures, the feared stimulus loses its ability to trigger extreme anxiety. CBT for anxiety typically lasts 12–20 sessions and is considered the gold standard psychotherapy.
fields.name: How CBT Treats Anxiety
fields.example: A person with social anxiety practices delivering a short speech to one trusted friend, then a small group, then a larger audience — each step reducing fear through repeated non-catastrophic exposure.

fields.name: Medications for Anxiety Disorders
fields.definition: Several medication classes are used to treat anxiety disorders. Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs, e.g., sertraline, escitalopram) and serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs, e.g., venlafaxine) are first-line medications — they reduce anxiety over several weeks by modulating neurotransmitter levels. Benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam, clonazepam) act rapidly by enhancing GABA activity but are typically used short-term due to dependence risk. Buspirone is a non-addictive anti-anxiety medication effective for GAD. Beta-blockers may address physical symptoms like racing heart in situational anxiety.
fields.example: A person with GAD starts sertraline (an SSRI) and notices reduced baseline worry after 4–6 weeks of consistent use.

fields.question: How common are anxiety disorders in the United States?
fields.answer: Anxiety disorders are the most common class of mental disorders in the US. An estimated 19.1% of US adults had an anxiety disorder in the past year. Among adolescents, the figure is even higher — approximately 31.9%. About one-third of US adults will experience an anxiety disorder at some point in their lifetime.

fields.question: What distinguishes a diagnosable anxiety disorder from everyday worry?
fields.answer: Everyday anxiety is proportionate to the situation, time-limited, and does not prevent normal functioning. A diagnosable anxiety disorder involves worry or fear that is excessive relative to the actual threat, is difficult or impossible to control, persists over time (weeks to months), occurs in multiple contexts, and causes meaningful impairment in work, relationships, or daily activities. The key factors are disproportionality, chronicity, and functional impact.

fields.question: How long must GAD symptoms persist for a diagnosis?
fields.answer: Symptoms of Generalized Anxiety Disorder must occur more days than not for at least 6 months and must be accompanied by at least three physical symptoms (such as muscle tension, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, or sleep disturbance) in adults.

fields.question: How long do panic attacks typically last?
fields.answer: Panic attacks typically peak within 10 minutes of onset and usually subside within 20–30 minutes, though they can feel much longer. Despite their intensity, panic attacks are not physically dangerous. The anticipation and fear of future attacks often cause more lasting disruption to daily life than the attacks themselves.

fields.question: Are PTSD and OCD classified as anxiety disorders?
fields.answer: No. Since the publication of DSM-5 in 2013, PTSD and OCD are no longer categorized as anxiety disorders. PTSD is now in its own chapter — Trauma- and Stressor-Related Disorders. OCD is now in the chapter Obsessive-Compulsive and Related Disorders. Both were previously grouped under anxiety disorders in DSM-IV, which is why they are still commonly associated with anxiety in public discussion.

fields.title: Anxiety and Physical Symptoms
fields.body: Anxiety disorders frequently produce significant physical symptoms that can be mistaken for medical conditions. Common physical manifestations include: racing or pounding heart, shortness of breath, chest tightness, muscle tension, headaches, fatigue, gastrointestinal distress (nausea, diarrhea, stomach cramps), sweating, trembling, dizziness, and dry mouth. These symptoms result from the chronic activation of the fight-or-flight stress response. Many people with anxiety disorders first seek help from a primary care physician for these physical complaints before receiving a mental health diagnosis. Treating the underlying anxiety disorder typically reduces physical symptoms as well.
